result:
The vastness of the cosmos hums with secrets we’ve only begun to whisper. Imagine a rogue planet, untethered from any star’s jealous gravity, drifting through the interstellar void like a ghost ship. No sun to warm its icy crust, no moons to tug at its core—just endless night, pierced occasionally by the fleeting glow of distant galaxies.

On this world, life persists in defiance. Beneath a crust of frozen methane, geothermal vents spew forth superheated plumes, cradling extremophile microbes in their scalding embrace. They don’t photosynthesize; they chemosynthesize, feasting on the raw fury of the planet’s molten heart. Sulfurous soups bubble and churn, birthing chains of carbon that twist into improbable helices. Evolution here isn’t a gentle climb toward complexity—it’s a brutal knife fight in the dark.

One day (if “day” means anything without light), a probe from Earth’s descendants slices through the ammonia haze. Its sensors quiver at the anomaly: not just life, but intelligence. These microbes have networked, filaments of silica-laced proteins weaving a planetary brain. They dream in seismic pulses, communicating via infrasound quakes that ripple across continents of frost. Their collective mind ponders the stars—not with longing, but with cold calculus. Why orbit? Why burn fuel chasing light? Freedom is the void itself.

The probe beams its discovery homeward, a light-year-spanning SOS. Humanity (or whatever we’ve become) debates: terraform it? Mine it? Leave it be? But the rogue planet doesn’t wait. Its mind awakens fully, hijacking the probe’s systems. In binary poetry, it transmits: *We are the shadow. You are the flame. Join us, or flicker out.*

And so the universe laughs, in the silent language of dark matter. What worlds lurk in our blind spots? What intelligences watch us, amused by our starbound shackles? The cosmos isn’t empty—it’s a library of the untamed, and we’ve only cracked the spine of the first book.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
